edges.description: Loss of ATGL co-activation prevents triacylglycerol hydrolysis, so TG droplets accumulate.
In parallel (ATGL-independently), loss of the ABHD5 epidermal lipid role impairs the skin barrier.
Hepatocyte triacylglycerol droplet accumulation produces hepatic steatosis.
Hepatic lipid storage enlarges the liver.
Progressive hepatic lipid storage can culminate in hepatic failure.
Lipid-droplet accumulation in skeletal muscle produces lipid-storage myopathy.
Systemic neutral lipid storage includes ocular involvement that can manifest as cataract.
Systemic neutral lipid storage includes ear involvement that can manifest as sensorineural hearing loss.
The ABHD5-dependent epidermal lipid-barrier defect produces the nonbullous congenital ichthyosis phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Mutations in ABHD5/CGI58 gene are associated with CDS

nodes.description: Biallelic ABHD5 (CGI-58) variants eliminate the lipid-droplet co-activator of adipose triglyceride lipase (ATGL); ABHD5 also has an ATGL-independent role in epidermal lipid metabolism underlying the ichthyosis.

evidence.reference_title: Neutral Lipid Storage Diseases: clinical/genetic features and natural history in a large cohort of Italian patients.
evidence.snippet: Neutral Lipid Storage Diseases (NLSDs: NLSD type M with Myopathy and NLSD type I with Ichthyosis)

nodes.description: Cataract reflects the ocular involvement of NLSD type I.

evidence.snippet:
The clinical phenotype involves multiple organs and systems,
including liver, eyes, ears, skeletal muscle and central nervous system (CNS)

nodes.description: ABHD5/CGI-58 has an ATGL-independent role in epidermal lipid metabolism (acylceramide synthesis via PNPLA1); its loss impairs the skin permeability barrier, underlying the ichthyosis that defines NLSD type I.

evidence.snippet:
severe skin permeability barrier defect
uncovers an essential ATGL-independent role of CGI-58 in skin lipid metabolism

nodes.description: Hepatic involvement that can progress to hepatic failure in NLSD type I.

evidence.reference_title: Neutral Lipid Storage Diseases: clinical/genetic features and natural history in a large cohort of Italian patients.
evidence.snippet: two patients (9.5%, both with NLSD-I) died of hepatic failure

nodes.description: Hepatic steatosis from intracellular triacylglycerol accumulation.

evidence.snippet:
The clinical phenotype involves multiple organs and systems,
including liver, eyes, ears, skeletal muscle and central nervous system (CNS)

nodes.description: Hepatomegaly accompanies the hepatic steatosis (a frequent visceral feature of NLSD-I).

evidence.snippet:
The clinical phenotype involves multiple organs and systems,
including liver, eyes, ears, skeletal muscle and central nervous system (CNS)

nodes.description: Congenital ichthyosis is the defining feature distinguishing NLSD type I from NLSD type M.

evidence.snippet: CDS patients present with a characteristic dermatological finding, ichthyosis, which is a non-bullous white scaling of the skin

evidence.reference_title: Neutral Lipid Storage Diseases: clinical/genetic features and natural history in a large cohort of Italian patients.
evidence.snippet: five (24%) lost their ability to walk or needed help when walking

nodes.description: Hearing impairment reflects involvement of the ears.

evidence.snippet:
The clinical phenotype involves multiple organs and systems,
including liver, eyes, ears, skeletal muscle and central nervous system (CNS)

nodes.description: Undegraded triacylglycerol accumulates as cytoplasmic lipid droplets in skin, liver, muscle, and leukocytes, driving the ichthyosis and visceral disease.

evidence.reference_title: Growth retardation, impaired triacylglycerol catabolism, hepatic steatosis, and lethal skin barrier defect in mice lacking comparative gene identification-58 (CGI-58).
evidence.snippet:
Mutations in both ATGL and CGI-58 cause "neutral lipid storage disease" 
characterized by massive accumulation of TG in various tissues
